title: I Wanted My Fiancé to Fight the Racist Men Who Harassed Me
description:
“Wyoming,” an excerpt from Good Woman by Savala Nolan We pulled off the freeway and into a parking lot because we were hungry and we had seen a sign for a franchise steakhouse glowing white against the sky. The parking lot was massive, outsize like so much of the middle of the country, and empty. […]

The post I Wanted My Fiancé to Fight the Racist Men Who Harassed Me</
hook: A visceral essay on desire, protection, and racism embedded in an intimate relationship moment.

title: The Timeless Provocations of “Wuthering Heights” (the Novel)
description: A great fuss surrounds Emerald Fennell’s anachronistic adaptation, but Emily Brontë’s ruthless text will always have the last word.
hook: How Emily Brontë's ruthless novel outlasts Emerald Fennell's adaptation—literary criticism as cultural analysis.

title: “I Wanted the Film to Have the Vignetted Existence of a Fable”: Sarmad Sultan Khoosat on his Genre-bending Berlinale Premiere Lali
description: A bullet grazes the shin of matriarch Sohni Ammi (Farazeh Syed) at her beloved son’s wedding. It was a celebratory bullet; shooting guns into the air replaces fireworks in this part of provincial Pakistan. Even though Sohni Ammi just needed st
hook: A director crafts genre-bending cinema as intimate fable—atmospheric filmmaking interrogated through craft.

title: Mitski – Nothing’s About to Happen to Me
description:
Mitski

Nothing’s About to Happen to Me

Mitski decamps to Nashville for an album of murder ballads that leans a little country

Nothing's About to Happen to Me by Mitski

Mitski has a talent for creating unique and unifying sounds for her albums. In the same way the acoustic arrangements of her previous album, 2023’s The Land Is Inhospit
hook: Mitski's Nashville murder ballads album reviewed—taking a major artist's genre pivot seriously and deeply.

title: Railsong by Rahul Bhattacharya review – a heartfelt tale of life on the Indian railways
description:
We follow one woman across decades of change in this deeply compassionate novel of independence and dreams

Indian Railways has been a source of patriotic pride, controversy, endless cover-ups, labyrinthine bureaucracy and death on an industrial scale since its founding in 1951. Rahul Bhattacharya’s Railsong, his first novel in 15 years since The Sly Company of People Who Care, explores its other m
hook: A novel about independence and dreams on Indian Railways—sensory, compassionate storytelling about transformation.
